comment:
The division-by-zero check uses `payload.b == 0` with a `float` operand. This will miss cases where `b` is a very small but non-zero float (no practical issue) but, more importantly, it will also miss the case where `b` is `-0.0` in Python: `float('-0.0') == 0` is `True`, so that edge case is actually handled. However, `float('inf')` or `float('nan')` passed as `b` are valid JSON floats and will not raise an error but will produce `inf` or `nan` as the result. Pydantic's `float` field accepts these by default. Consider using `pydantic.Field(allow_inf_nan=False)` on the `a` and `b` fields, or adding explicit validation, to avoid returning non-finite results to callers.
```suggestion
    a: float = Field(..., description="Left operand", allow_inf_nan=False)
    b: float = Field(..., description="Right operand", allow_inf_nan=False)
```

The `CalcResponse.op` field is typed as plain `str` rather than the same `Literal["add", "sub", "mul", "div"]` used in `CalcRequest`. This means the response model's OpenAPI schema will document `op` as an arbitrary string instead of the restricted set of valid operations, which is inconsistent and gives API consumers less information. Consider reusing the same `Literal` type (or a shared type alias) for `op` in `CalcResponse`.

The README only provides Windows/PowerShell setup and run instructions (using `.\.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1` and `Invoke-RestMethod`). There are no instructions for Linux/macOS users (e.g., `source .venv/bin/activate` and `curl`). This limits the accessibility of the documentation for developers on other platforms.
The PR title "CALCUALTOR API" contains a spelling error Ă˘â‚¬â€ť "CALCUALTOR" should be "CALCULATOR". While the title is metadata rather than code, it's worth correcting before merge.

The "## Run" section uses a PowerShell code fence but the command `uvicorn app.main:app --reload` works on all platforms (Linux, macOS, and Windows). It would be clearer to either use a generic/bash fence or add platform-specific equivalents the same way the "## Setup" section does, to avoid misleading Linux/macOS users into thinking the run command is Windows-only.

Division by zero is only checked when `payload.b == 0`, but due to floating-point arithmetic, a divisor very close to zero (like `1e-308`) could produce `inf` as a result. While `allow_inf_nan=False` on the inputs correctly rejects infinite/NaN inputs, the computed `result` is a plain `float` in `CalcResponse` with no such constraint Ă˘â‚¬â€ť so an extremely small divisor like `5e-324` (the smallest positive float) would produce an `inf` result that would be serialized and returned to the caller without error. Consider either adding `allow_inf_nan=False` to the `result` field of `CalcResponse`, or checking `math.isinf(result)` after division and raising an appropriate error.

The `load_comment_ids` function calls `int(i)` on each command-line argument without any error handling. If a user accidentally passes a non-numeric argument (e.g., a typo like `"abc"`), this will raise an unhandled `ValueError` with a cryptic Python traceback rather than a clean user-facing error message. Consider wrapping the conversion in a try/except and printing an informative error before calling `sys.exit(1)`.
```suggestion
        ids = set()
        for i in args:
            try:
                ids.add(int(i))
            except ValueError:
                print(f"ERROR: Invalid comment ID '{i}'. Expected an integer.", file=sys.stderr)
                sys.exit(1)
        return ids
```

The `THREADS_QUERY` fetches at most 100 review threads (`first: 100`) and at most 100 comments per thread (`first: 100`) with no pagination. For large PRs with more than 100 review threads, or threads with more than 100 comments, the script will silently miss threads and fail to resolve them. The GitHub GraphQL API supports cursor-based pagination (`pageInfo { endCursor hasNextPage }`) which should be used here to handle all threads correctly.
